entries.title: What hidden parameters are
entries.body:
Hidden (undocumented) parameters are request parameters that the application processes on the backend but never exposes in the interface: they are absent from forms, links and visible JS. Fuzzing helps discover them, and a hidden parameter once found often turns out to be vulnerable to a wide range of attacks (IDOR, SSRF, injections, authorization bypass, toggling debug modes).

The idea is simple: the application "listens" for more than it shows. Developers leave debug, legacy and service parameters behind, forget to remove them, or assume nobody knows about them. Brute-forcing them with a wordlist and comparing the server's reaction is a separate recon technique.

entries.title: Where to look for hidden parameters
entries.body:
• Any endpoint that accepts something: pages, APIs, forms, export/import, PDF and email generation.
• Old versions of pages and URLs - via the Wayback Machine and archives (the parameter was removed from the UI long ago, but the backend handler remains).
• JS files: look for parameter names in the frontend code, including commented-out and unused branches.
• Points where you see a difference in behavior when the input changes: different response, length, status, response time, new fields in the JSON.

How to test: brute-force parameter names with a wordlist and compare the response with the baseline (without the parameter). A difference in the response code, body length, error text or timing is a signal that the parameter is "live".

entries.title: Tools
entries.body:
• PortSwigger/param-miner - a Burp extension for finding hidden, unlinked parameters and headers.
• s0md3v/Arjun - a toolset for discovering HTTP parameters.
• Sh1Yo/x8 - a toolset for discovering hidden parameters.
• tomnomnom/waybackurls - pulls all URLs known to the Wayback Machine for a domain.
• devanshbatham/ParamSpider - mines URLs (and parameters) from web archives for bug hunting and fuzzing.

entries.title: How parameter brute-force works
entries.body: You take a wordlist of common parameter names and send them to the endpoint, watching for unexpected backend behavior: a change in the response code, body length, the appearance of new data or errors. Any difference from the baseline request is a candidate for a hidden parameter that you then verify manually.

entries.title: Brute-forcing with Arjun
entries.body:
arjun -u "https://example.com/"
arjun -u "https://example.com/" -m POST
arjun -u "https://example.com/" -m JSON
arjun -u "https://example.com/" -w params.txt

entries.title: Brute-forcing with ffuf (via FUZZ in the parameter name)
entries.body:
ffuf -u "https://example.com/?FUZZ=test" -w params.txt -fs <baseline-size>
ffuf -u "https://example.com/" -X POST -d "FUZZ=test" -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" -w params.txt -fs <baseline-size>

entries.title: Parameter name wordlists
entries.body:
Arjun: db/large.txt, db/medium.txt, db/small.txt - https://github.com/s0md3v/Arjun (arjun/db directory)
samlists: sam-cc-parameters-lowercase-all.txt, sam-cc-parameters-mixedcase-all.txt - https://github.com/the-xentropy/samlists
SecLists: Discovery/Web-Content/burp-parameter-names.txt

entries.title: Common hidden parameter names
entries.body:
debug
test
admin
source
edit
grant
token
admin_only
debug_mode
include
file
url
callback
redirect
preview
enable
disable
mode
role
user
user_id
account
is_admin
show
verbose
trace
format

entries.title: Why try debug / test / admin / source
entries.body:
Some parameter names enable "service" behavior more often than others, and they are worth trying first:
• debug / debug_mode / verbose / trace - extended output, stack traces, variable dumps, disabling some checks.
• test - test code branches, mock data, weakened checks.
• admin / is_admin / role / grant - an attempt to escalate privileges or enable admin mode (often paired with mass assignment).
• source / view-source / raw - returning the page/template source instead of rendering it.

Try different values: 1, true, on, yes, debug, and also an empty value.

entries.title: Where to try the parameter - GET / POST / JSON / header
entries.body:
The server may accept the same hidden parameter from different places - check every channel:
• GET - in the query string: ?param=value.
• POST (form) - in the application/x-www-form-urlencoded body: param=value.
• JSON - a key in the application/json body: {"param":"value"}.
• Header - a custom HTTP header: X-Param: value (especially X-Forwarded-For, X-Original-URL, X-Rewrite-URL, X-Debug).

Sometimes the parameter is ignored in GET but works in the JSON body or in a header - so the brute-force is worth repeating for each method.

entries.title: Examples of passing one parameter in different ways
entries.body:
GET /endpoint?debug=true HTTP/1.1
Host: example.com

---

POST /endpoint HTTP/1.1
Host: example.com
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

debug=true

---

POST /endpoint HTTP/1.1
Host: example.com
Content-Type: application/json

{"debug": true}

---

GET /endpoint HTTP/1.1
Host: example.com
X-Debug: true

entries.title: Old parameters from web archives and JS
entries.body:
Hidden parameters often do not need to be guessed - they can be pulled from history and code:
• Wayback Machine (http://web.archive.org/) - old versions of pages with parameters already removed from the UI.
• JS files - go through the frontend code and find parameter names, including commented-out and unused ones.
• waybackurls / ParamSpider - automatically collect URLs (and parameters) from web archives for further fuzzing.

Add the names collected this way to your brute-force wordlist - they are more accurate than generic lists.
